sanskrit_devanagari:
यावानर्थ उदपाने सर्वतः सम्प्लुतोदके ।
तावान्सर्वेषु वेदेषु ब्राह्मणस्य विजानतः ॥ ४६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yāvān artha uda-pāne sarvataḥ samplutodake |
tāvān sarveṣu vedeṣu brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ || 46 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | rather than double danda ॥ as terminator; spelling 'sarvataḥ' identical)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.46।। rather than ॥ ४६ ॥)
IAST exact (Devanagari not displayed on this page; IAST body matches: 'yāvān artha udapāne sarvataḥ samplutodake tāvān sarveṣu vedeṣu brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.046 = MBh_06,024.046). Body text identical: 'yāvān artha udapāne sarvataḥ saṃplutodake / tāvān sarveṣu vedeṣu brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ'. Note: GRETIL uses 'saṃplutodake' (anusvara before 'p') where vedabase prints 'sampluta' (assimilated nasal); these are equivalent renderings of the same conjunct under Sanskrit orthographic conventions.

anvaya.meaning: in all respects / from all sides
in a great flooded reservoir of water
in the Vedas / in Vedic literatures
of the brahmana / of one who knows Brahman
of the one who has fully realized / who knows

translations.verbatim_quote: All purposes served by a small well can at once be served by a great reservoir of water. Similarly, all the purposes of the Vedas can be served to one who knows the purpose behind them.
Whatever purpose is served by a small well of water is naturally served in all respects by a large lake. Similarly, one who realizes the Absolute Truth also fulfills the purpose of all the Vedas.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, 320 chars of 2940-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The rituals and sacrifices mentioned in the karma-kāṇḍa division of the Vedic literature are meant to encourage gradual development of self-realization. And the purpose of self-realization is clearly stated in the Fifteenth Chapter of the Bhagavad-gītā (15.15): the purpose of studying the Vedas is to know Lord Kṛṣṇa, t

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Shankara bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In the world, yavan, whatever; arthah, utility, use, like bathing, drinking, etc.; one has udapane, in a well, pond and other numerous limited reservoirs; all that, indeed, is achieved, i.e. all those needs are fulfilled to that very extent; sampluhtodake, when there is a flood; sarvatah, all around.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse strictly: ritual injunctions of the Vedas serve the unrealized; once the sage has direct knowledge of Brahman, those injunctions become as a small water-pond before a flood. Crucially, Shankara does NOT say the Vedas are useless — he says the brahmana who has not yet realized still needs them, and only the realized one (vijānataḥ) is past their utility. Before knowledge, one performs prescribed duty per qualification.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: summary-only (raw text behind JS-rendered page; only paraphrased fragments returned)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja's reading is materially close to Shankara's at the level of analogy — small well : flood :: ritual fruits : knowledge of the Supreme — but the Vishishtadvaita frame insists the realized one's relation to the Veda is not abandonment but inclusion: the ritual ends are subsumed within devotion to the Supreme Person, not nullified.

positions.rendering: to one who knows the purpose behind them (i.e., the realized brahmana)
one who realizes the Absolute Truth
the brahmana who has direct knowledge of Brahman; emphasis on jñāna-niṣṭha as the qualifier
one who knows the Supreme Person; ritual ends are subsumed, not nullified

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The compound brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ scopes the verse. Shankara reads it as the jñāna-niṣṭha brahmana for whom rituals are now redundant; Ramanuja reads the same scope but reframes 'redundant' as 'subsumed within devotion to the Supreme'; Prabhupada flattens to 'one who knows the purpose behind them', a phrasing acceptable to all traditions but losing the specifically jñāna-vs-bhakti tension; Mukundananda renders 'realizes the Absolute Truth', similarly tradition-neutral. The verse is therefore not a blanket dismissal of the Vedas — its scope is the realized one (vijānataḥ); the unrealized still needs the small water-pond.

Orthographic variant only — both spellings represent the same Sanskrit conjunct under different scholarly conventions; no semantic difference. Logged here for transparency on Sanskrit-source agreement, not as a translator disagreement.

literal_meaning: As much purpose as a small well of water serves, all of that is served by a great flood reservoir; similarly, of all the Vedas, that much is the use to the brahmana who has realized.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna establishes that scripture is provisional. The Vedic rituals and prescribed duties are the small well — they serve real needs for the unrealized seeker. For the brahmana who has reached direct realization (vijānataḥ), those same scriptures collapse to a small pond before the flood of realized knowledge. Critically: this is not a rejection of the Vedas. The verse is scoped — its referent is the realized one, not the seeker still en route. Shankara reads the realized state as jñāna-niṣṭha (steadfastness in Knowledge); Ramanuja reframes as devotion to the Supreme that subsumes the ritual ends. Both agree the unrealized brahmana still needs the small well.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full bhashya verbatim — only the translator-rendered excerpt was machine-extractable; full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled.
Ramanuja's commentary verbatim — page renders commentary in JS, so only paraphrase recovered.
Madhva's Dvaita commentary not pulled — would require a separate source.
